title: Gout
oneLiner: Acute crystal arthritis from monosodium urate deposition; typical in middle-aged men/postmenopausal women; mimics septic arthritis – aspirate joint to rule out infection.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic arthritis** – The single finding: **joint aspirate with positive gram stain or >50,000 WBC/µL (or frankly purulent fluid)**. Do NOT start colchicine/NSAIDs until aspirate results rule out sepsis.
- **Acute urate nephropathy / AKI** – Rare but can cause oliguria with rising creatinine; suspect if massive diuresis from rapid urate mobilisation (e.g. tumour lysis). Check U+Es immediately; if creatinine rising + hyperuricaemia > 0.6 mmol/L, start rashburicase/urate oxidase [check local protocol] and nephrology.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** severe acute monoarticular pain (often 1st MTP – podagra), redness, heat, swelling, exquisite tenderness – patient cannot bear anything touching the joint.
- **Key exam findings:** erythema over joint, effusion, limited range of motion due to pain. May have tophi on ears/elbows/heels in chronic cases. Fever may be present but low-grade.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Septic arthritis** – Systemic unwell, high fever, recent joint surgery/IVDU. Aspirate gram stain + culture is definitive.
- **Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate deposition)** – Often knee/wrist; less intense. Synovial fluid shows rhomboid crystals (positive birefringence under polarised light).
- **Cellulitis** – No joint effusion, fever + erythema spreading, no joint pain on passive movement.
- **Traumatic arthritis** – History of injury, no crystals on aspiration.
- **Psoriatic / reactive arthritis** – Typically oligoarticular, dactylitis, skin/nail changes.
- **Splitter:** **Joint aspiration**: positive monosodium urate crystals = gout; positive gram stain/culture = septic; no crystals + high WBC = consider pseudogout or septic.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Joint aspiration (if skilled – otherwise delay until senior/ultrasound). Assess NEWS2, pain score.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WBC/CRP elevated in both gout and septic), U+E (for renal function), urate (may be high but not diagnostic – can be normal in acute flare), CRP, consider blood cultures if fevers.
- **Imaging:** 
  - First-line: X-ray of affected joint – may show soft tissue swelling, but no acute changes. Bony erosions with overhanging edges in chronic tophaceous gout.
  - Ultrasound: double contour sign (urate deposition on cartilage) – can help confirm, especially in atypical joints.
- **Special tests:** Synovial fluid analysis – polarised light microscopy for negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals. Also gram stain, culture, cell count.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **American College of Rheumatology (ACR)/EULAR criteria for gout** – e.g., presence of MSU crystals OR typical clinical features + hyperuricaemia + rapid response to colchicine/NSAIDs. For junior: if you see crystals, it's gout.
- **Never-miss test:** **Joint aspiration with gram stain and culture** – missing septic arthritis can be limb/life-threatening.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess and treat pain: consider paracetamol, plus appropriate anti-inflammatory as below (but hold until septic arthritis excluded if high suspicion).
  - If septic arthritis excluded: start acute flare therapy.
  - Rest and elevate affected joint. Ice packs for comfort.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue chosen acute therapy for 5-7 days or until flare resolves.
  - Review for ULT initiation (consider start after flare fully resolved, but if patient was already on ULT, do not stop it – continue and add acute cover).
  - Check renal function; if CKD, avoid NSAIDs.
  - Monitor for adverse effects of therapy (e.g., diarrhoea with colchicine, GI/renal issues with NSAIDs).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - ULT indication: recurrent flares (>2/year), tophi, chronic kidney disease stage ≥3, or erosive changes on X-ray.
  - First-line ULT: allopurinol – start at low dose 100mg daily, titrate up every 2-4 weeks aiming for serum urate < 0.36 mmol/L (or <0.30 mmol/L if tophi). Consider HLA-B*5801 testing in high-risk ethnic groups (e.g. Han Chinese, Thai, Korean, African-American) – if positive, avoid allopurinol (use febuxostat).
  - Febuxostat: alternative if allopurinol intolerant or contraindicated (but cautions in CVD).
  - ULT initiation should be after acute flare has settled (usually 1-2 weeks post-resolution) AND with prophylaxis (colchicine 500mcg BD or NSAID low dose for 3-6 months).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV N Saline if dehydrated or unable to tolerate oral (otherwise encourage oral fluids).
  - VTE prophylaxis: assess risk – pharmacological if appropriate.
  - Diet: encourage low-purine diet, but acute management not dependent on diet.
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2 if febrile or on high-dose NSAIDs/colchicine (monitor for toxicity).
  - Analgesia: regular paracetamol as base; acute flare therapy as prescribed.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line acute (choose one based on comorbidities):**
  - **NSAID** (e.g. naproxen 500mg BD or [check local policy] – avoid if eGFR <30, active GI bleed, heart failure, on anticoagulants. Use with PPI if high GI risk.
  - **Colchicine** – 500mcg 2-3 times daily; max cumulative dose 6mg per course. Caution: diarrhoea, vomiting; reduce dose in renal impairment (e.g. eGFR <30: max 500mcg once daily, or avoid). Check BNF.
  - **Steroids** – If NSAID/colchicine contraindicated: prednisolone 30-40mg OD for 5-7 days. Or intra-articular steroid (methylprednisolone 40mg) if single joint.
- **Second-line / alternative:** Corticosteroids (oral or IA) as above; ACTH is rarely used.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - NSAIDs + anticoagulants/antiplatelets: increased bleeding risk.
  - Colchicine + strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g. clarithromycin, ketoconazole) – risk of toxicity; avoid.
  - Colch

rawMarkdown:
## Gout
**One-liner:** Acute crystal arthritis from monosodium urate deposition; typical in middle-aged men/postmenopausal women; mimics septic arthritis – aspirate joint to rule out infection.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic arthritis** – The single finding: **joint aspirate with positive gram stain or >50,000 WBC/µL (or frankly purulent fluid)**. Do NOT start colchicine/NSAIDs until aspirate results rule out sepsis.
- **Acute urate nephropathy / AKI** – Rare but can cause oliguria with rising creatinine; suspect if massive diuresis from rapid urate mobilisation (e.g. tumour lysis). Check U+Es immediately; if creatinine rising + hyperuricaemia > 0.6 mmol/L, start rashburicase/urate oxidase [check local protocol] and nephrology.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** severe acute monoarticular pain (often 1st MTP – podagra), redness, heat, swelling, exquisite tenderness – patient cannot bear anything touching the joint.
- **Key exam findings:** erythema over joint, effusion, limited range of motion due to pain. May have tophi on ears/elbows/heels in chronic cases. Fever may be present but low-grade.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Septic arthritis** – Systemic unwell, high fever, recent joint surgery/IVDU. Aspirate gram stain + culture is definitive.
- **Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate deposition)** – Often knee/wrist; less intense. Synovial fluid shows rhomboid crystals (positive birefringence under polarised light).
- **Cellulitis** – No joint effusion, fever + erythema spreading, no joint pain on passive movement.
- **Traumatic arthritis** – History of injury, no crystals on aspiration.
- **Psoriatic / reactive arthritis** – Typically oligoarticular, dactylitis, skin/nail changes.
- **Splitter:** **Joint aspiration**: positive monosodium urate crystals = gout; positive gram stain/culture = septic; no crystals + high WBC = consider pseudogout or septic.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Joint aspiration (if skilled – otherwise delay until senior/ultrasound). Assess NEWS2, pain score.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WBC/CRP elevated in both gout and septic), U+E (for renal function), urate (may be high but not diagnostic – can be normal in acute flare), CRP, consider blood cultures if fevers.
- **Imaging:** 
  - First-line: X-ray of affected joint – may show soft tissue swelling, but no acute changes. Bony erosions with overhanging edges in chronic tophaceous gout.
  - Ultrasound: double contour sign (urate deposition on cartilage) – can help confirm, especially in atypical joints.
- **Special tests:** Synovial fluid analysis – polarised light microscopy for negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals. Also gram stain, culture, cell count.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **American College of Rheumatology (ACR)/EULAR criteria for gout** – e.g., presence of MSU crystals OR typical clinical features + hyperuricaemia + rapid response to colchicine/NSAIDs. For junior: if you see crystals, it's gout.
- **Never-miss test:** **Joint aspiration with gram stain and culture** – missing septic arthritis can be limb/life-threatening.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess and treat pain: consider paracetamol, plus appropriate anti-inflammatory as below (but hold until septic arthritis excluded if high suspicion).
  - If septic arthritis excluded: start acute flare therapy.
  - Rest and elevate affected joint. Ice packs for comfort.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue chosen acute therapy for 5-7 days or until flare resolves.
  - Review for ULT initiation (consider start after flare fully resolved, but if patient was already on ULT, do not stop it – continue and add acute cover).
  - Check renal function; if CKD, avoid NSAIDs.
  - Monitor for adverse effects of therapy (e.g., diarrhoea with colchicine, GI/renal issues with NSAIDs).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - ULT indication: recurrent flares (>2/year), tophi, chronic kidney disease stage ≥3, or erosive changes on X-ray.
  - First-line ULT: allopurinol – start at low dose 100mg daily, titrate up every 2-4 weeks aiming for serum urate < 0.36 mmol/L (or <0.30 mmol/L if tophi). Consider HLA-B*5801 testing in high-risk ethnic groups (e.g. Han Chinese, Thai, Korean, African-American) – if positive, avoid allopurinol (use febuxostat).
  - Febuxostat: alternative if allopurinol intolerant or contraindicated (but cautions in CVD).
  - ULT initiation should be after acute flare has settled (usually 1-2 weeks post-resolution) AND with prophylaxis (colchicine 500mcg BD or NSAID low dose for 3-6 months).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV N Saline if dehydrated or unable to tolerate oral (otherwise encourage oral fluids).
  - VTE prophylaxis: assess risk – pharmacological if appropriate.
  - Diet: encourage low-purine diet, but acute management not dependent on diet.
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2 if febrile or on high-dose NSAIDs/colchicine (monitor for toxicity).
  - Analgesia: regular paracetamol as base; acute flare therapy as prescribed.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line acute (choose one based on comorbidities):**
  - **NSAID** (e.g. naproxen 500mg BD or [check local policy] – avoid if eGFR <30, active GI bleed, heart failure, on anticoagulants. Use with PPI if high GI risk.
  - **Colchicine** – 500mcg 2-3 times daily; max cumulative dose 6mg per course. Caution: diarrhoea, vomiting; reduce dose in renal impairment (e.g. eGFR <30: max 500mcg once daily, or avoid). Check BNF.
  - **Steroids** – If NSAID/colchicine contraindicated: prednisolone 30-40mg OD for 5-7 days. Or intra-articular steroid (methylprednisolone 40mg) if single joint.
- **Second-line / alternative:** Corticosteroids (oral or IA) as above; ACTH is rarely used.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - NSAIDs + anticoagulants/antiplatelets: increased bleeding risk.
  - Colchicine + strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g. clarithromycin, ketoconazole) – risk of toxicity; avoid.
  - Colch
